column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). MDF Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/wood/hardwood/mdf-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for MDF surface cleaning without charring
Near-IR wavelength for optimal MDF absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control
High repetition rate for efficient surface cleaning
Energy density threshold for MDF surface ablation
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled material removal
Optimal scanning speed for uniform surface treatment
Number of cleaning passes for thorough contamination removal
Beam overlap percentage for complete surface coverage
Beam dwell time per spot for effective material removal

Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of wood-based materials like MDF to avoid charring; starting at 100W for light contamination. Max safe before substrate damage (thermal degradation) is ~500W at 1064 nm. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on laser ablation of composites (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective speeds for contamination removal on MDF; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s) to ensure ablation, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light. Recommended 1500 mm/s balances efficiency and heat control. Source: Industry manuals from CleanLaser and process optimization in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for MDF cleaning due to cost-effectiveness; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ but unnecessary for most industrial apps. 20 ns minimizes thermal effects on low-conductivity MDF. Source: Laser physics reviews in Appl. Phys. A (2019) on pulsed laser cleaning of organics.

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning; 50 kHz recommended for MDF to achieve fast coverage without excessive heat accumulation, trading off speed vs. thermal buildup in resinous material. Higher rates (>80 kHz) risk damage. Source: Academic literature, e.g., J. Appl. Phys. (2021) on pulse repetition in ablation.

Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants from MDF; damage threshold ~5-10 J/cm² due to charring/melting (MDF decomposition ~200-300°C). Recommended 2 J/cm² for effective cleaning below threshold. Source: Ablation studies in Wood Sci. Technol. (2017) and laser threshold measurements.

Beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for MDF to balance resolution on fibrous surface and large-area coverage, avoiding excessive heat concentration. Finer for details (<150 μm), larger for broad prep (300-500 μm). Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf lasers.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete MDF surface prep without overexposure. Source: Industry case studies in laser cleaning of wood composites (e.g., Proc. SPIE 2022).

Ensures uniform coverage on irregular MDF surfaces; 60% recommended for optimal cleaning without gaps or over-ablation, typical range 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization in J. Mater. Process. Technol. (2019).
